graph_vector_fulltext.message:
Hello! Based on the provided EU AI Act excerpt, the overlapping obligations/constraints in that scenario are:

- High-risk classification: AI systems used for monitoring or evaluation of persons in work-related contractual relationships are classified as high-risk, so the employer deploying such a system is deploying a “high-risk AI system”.

- Worker information rights (and consultation where applicable): it remains necessary to ensure workers and their representatives are informed about the planned deployment of high-risk AI systems in the workplace where information / information-and-consultation obligations in other legal instruments are not fulfilled. This information right is described as ancillary and necessary to the objective of protecting fundamental rights.

- Deployer obligations to inform affected natural persons: the deployer should inform natural persons about the use of the high-risk AI system, including (i) the intended purpose and (ii) the type of decisions it makes. The deployer should also inform them about their right to an explanation under the Regulation.

- Human oversight / competence (deployer-side): persons dealing with the operation/use of AI should have an adequate level of AI literacy, including necessary training and authority, to properly fulfil their tasks (this is framed as needed for proper understanding/compliance). The deployer is expected to understand how the AI system works and interpret its outputs to support informed decision-making.

- Preservation of existing labour law: the Regulation is “without prejudice” to obligations for employers under Union or national law and practice to inform or to inform and consult workers or their representatives (including Directive 2002/14/EC). In other words, AI Act information duties do not replace or reduce existing labour-law information/consultation duties; they sit alongside them.

- Right to explanation (and the duty to flag it): natural persons have a “right to an explanation” (right to obtain an explanation is noted as potentially limited by Union law), and in this workplace high-risk deployment context the deployer should inform workers/affected persons that this right exists.
graph_vector_fulltext.metric_question: An employer deploys a high-risk AI system for monitoring employee performance and allocating tasks. What are the overlapping obligations from: the high-risk classification, worker information rights, deployer obligations, human oversight, existing labour law preservation, and the right to explanation?
graph_vector_fulltext.metric_answer:
Hello! Based on the provided EU AI Act excerpt, the overlapping obligations/constraints in that scenario are:

- High-risk classification: AI systems used for monitoring or evaluation of persons in work-related contractual relationships are classified as high-risk, so the employer deploying such a system is deploying a “high-risk AI system”.

- Worker information rights (and consultation where applicable): it remains necessary to ensure workers and their representatives are informed about the planned deployment of high-risk AI systems in the workplace where information / information-and-consultation obligations in other legal instruments are not fulfilled. This information right is described as ancillary and necessary to the objective of protecting fundamental rights.

- Deployer obligations to inform affected natural persons: the deployer should inform natural persons about the use of the high-risk AI system, including (i) the intended purpose and (ii) the type of decisions it makes. The deployer should also inform them about their right to an explanation under the Regulation.

- Human oversight / competence (deployer-side): persons dealing with the operation/use of AI should have an adequate level of AI literacy, including necessary training and authority, to properly fulfil their tasks (this is framed as needed for proper understanding/compliance). The deployer is expected to understand how the AI system works and interpret its outputs to support informed decision-making.

- Preservation of existing labour law: the Regulation is “without prejudice” to obligations for employers under Union or national law and practice to inform or to inform and consult workers or their representatives (including Directive 2002/14/EC). In other words, AI Act information duties do not replace or reduce existing labour-law information/consultation duties; they sit alongside them.

- Right to explanation (and the duty to flag it): natural persons have a “right to an explanation” (right to obtain an explanation is noted as potentially limited by Union law), and in this workplace high-risk deployment context the deployer should inform workers/affected persons that this right exists.

It remains necessary to ensure  information of workers and their representatives on the planned deployment of high-risk AI systems at the  workplace where the conditions for those information or information and consultation obligations in other legal  instruments are not fulfilled. Moreover, such information right is ancillary and necessary to the objective of  protecting fundamental rights that underlies this
----
 the high-risk AI system. This information should include the  intended purpose and the type of decisions it makes. The deployer should also inform the natural persons about  their right to an explanation provided under this Regulation. With regard to high-risk AI systems used for law  enforcement purposes, that obligation should be implemented in accordance with Article 13 of Directive (EU)  2016/680. (94) Any processing of biometric data involved in the use of AI systems for biometric identification for
----
competence, in particular an adequate level of AI literacy, training and authority to properly fulfil those tasks. Those  obligations should be without prejudice to other deployer obligations in relation to high-risk AI systems under  Union or national law. (92) This Regulation is without prejudice to obligations for employers to inform or to inform and consult workers or  their representatives under Union or national law and practice, including Directive 2002/14/EC of the European  Parliament and of the Council (39),
----
 in relation to this Regulation with respect to specific categories of high-risk AI systems that are  found to present a serious risk across two or more Member States. Joint activities to promote compliance should be  carried out in accordance with Article 9 of Regulation (EU) 2019/1020. The AI Office should provide coordination  support for joint investigations. (161) It is necessary to clarify the responsibilities and competences at Union and national level as regards AI systems that  are built on general-purpose AI

chunks.text: , including Directive 2002/14/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council (39), on decisions to put into service or use AI systems. It remains necessary to ensure information of workers and their representatives on the planned deployment of high-risk AI systems at the workplace where the conditions for those information or information and consultation obligations in other legal instruments are not fulfilled. Moreover, such information right is ancillary and necessary to the objective of protecting fundamental rights that underlies this

the high-risk AI system. This information should include the intended purpose and the type of decisions it makes. The deployer should also inform the natural persons about their right to an explanation provided under this Regulation. With regard to high-risk AI systems used for law enforcement purposes, that obligation should be implemented in accordance with Article 13 of Directive (EU) 2016/680. (94) Any processing of biometric data involved in the use of AI systems for biometric identification for

competence, in particular an adequate level of AI literacy, training and authority to properly fulfil those tasks. Those obligations should be without prejudice to other deployer obligations in relation to high-risk AI systems under Union or national law. (92) This Regulation is without prejudice to obligations for employers to inform or to inform and consult workers or their representatives under Union or national law and practice, including Directive 2002/14/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council (39),

in relation to this Regulation with respect to specific categories of high-risk AI systems that are found to present a serious risk across two or more Member States. Joint activities to promote compliance should be carried out in accordance with Article 9 of Regulation (EU) 2019/1020. The AI Office should provide coordination support for joint investigations. (161) It is necessary to clarify the responsibilities and competences at Union and national level as regards AI systems that are built on general-purpose AI
